skill.name: gene-database
skill.description: Query NCBI Gene through official E-utilities and Datasets APIs for gene search, metadata retrieval, and batch annotation workflows.
skill.summary: Search NCBI Gene and retrieve gene metadata, identifiers, locations, annotations, and batch lookup results.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, credential, and weak-crypto patterns, but review found no malicious intent or prompt injection. Most command and blocker alerts are documentation or substring false positives; the real residual risks are expected NCBI network access, executable scripts, CLI API-key handling, and optional local file input or output.

medium_findings.title: Expected Network Requests to NCBI APIs
medium_findings.description: The scripts make outbound HTTPS requests to NCBI E-utilities and NCBI Datasets endpoints. This is required for the skill, but users should know that gene queries and optional API keys are sent to NCBI.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network endpoints are hardcoded official NCBI domains and match the skill purpose. I found no evidence of hidden third-party exfiltration or suspicious domains.

low_findings.title: CLI API Key Exposure Risk
low_findings.description: The scripts accept NCBI API keys through command-line arguments. This is normal for simple CLI tools, but keys can be visible in shell history or process listings on shared systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API-key parameters are clearly present in CLI parsers. The keys are only sent to NCBI, so this is exposure hygiene rather than malicious credential theft.

low_findings.title: User-Controlled Local File Read and Write
low_findings.description: The batch lookup script can read gene identifiers from a user-provided file and write JSON results to a user-provided output path. This is expected behavior, but users should choose trusted paths.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct file read and write operations are present and controlled by CLI arguments. I found no evidence of unauthorized file traversal, persistence, or broad filesystem scanning.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Command Alerts Are Documentation
low_findings.description: The many external-command alerts in markdown files are examples and inline code spans, not executable Ruby or shell backtick execution. They document how to run the included scripts.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are inside markdown documentation and fenced examples. They are not code paths executed by the skill runtime.

low_findings.title: Weak-Crypto and Reconnaissance Alerts Are Substring Matches
low_findings.description: The blocker alerts correspond to benign words such as description, Datasets API, status codes, and gene metadata examples. No cryptographic API, system enumeration, or reconnaissance behavior was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found no imports or calls for weak hashing, encryption, host discovery, or system inspection. The scanner appears to match benign text fragments.

content.value_statement: Gene research often requires precise identifiers, annotations, and organism context from trusted sources. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code query NCBI Gene and organize results for analysis.
actual_capabilities: Search NCBI Gene by symbol, name, organism, disease term, pathway, or Gene ID.
Retrieve gene summaries, chromosomal locations, aliases, organism details, and selected RefSeq metadata.
Use NCBI E-utilities for search, summary, fetch, link, and database information workflows.
Use NCBI Datasets API for direct gene metadata retrieval by ID or symbol and taxon.
Process batches of gene symbols or IDs with built-in rate limiting and JSON output.
Reference documented query patterns for disease discovery, annotation, ortholog review, and publication mining.
limitations: Requires internet access to NCBI services and depends on NCBI availability and response formats.
Does not perform variant calling, sequence alignment, pathway enrichment, or clinical interpretation.
API keys are passed through command-line arguments, which may be visible on shared systems.
Results reflect public NCBI Gene records and may require expert review for research conclusions.

use_cases.title: Validate disease gene candidates
use_cases.description: Search public NCBI Gene records for candidate genes and collect identifiers, names, locations, and organism context.

use_cases.title: Annotate gene panels
use_cases.description: Batch lookup gene symbols or IDs and prepare consistent metadata for downstream tables and reports.

use_cases.title: Explore gene records
use_cases.description: Compare gene symbols, aliases, map locations, and database links while learning NCBI query syntax.

prompt_templates.title: Basic gene lookup
prompt_templates.scenario: Find one gene in one organism.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the gene-database skill to search for BRCA1 in human. Show the Gene ID, description, organism, chromosome, and map location.

prompt_templates.title: Batch annotation request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the batch lookup workflow for TP53, EGFR, KRAS, and BRCA1 in human. Return a concise table with identifiers and locations.

prompt_templates.title: Disease-focused search
prompt_templates.scenario: Find genes linked to a phenotype or disease term.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search NCBI Gene for human genes associated with diabetes. Limit results to 20 and summarize symbols, Gene IDs, and descriptions.

prompt_templates.title: Advanced workflow planning
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a reproducible NCBI Gene annotation workflow for a 300-gene panel, including rate limits, API key handling, output format, and validation steps.

output_examples.input: Annotate TP53, EGFR, and KRAS for human.
output: TP53: tumor protein p53, Gene ID 7157, chromosome 17.
EGFR: epidermal growth factor receptor, Gene ID 1956, chromosome 7.
KRAS: KRAS proto-oncogene, Gene ID 3845, chromosome 12.

output_examples.input: Find genes related to apoptosis in mouse.
output: Search results include genes matching the selected biological process and organism filter.
Each result can include Gene ID, official symbol, description, organism, chromosome, and database links.

best_practices: Always include organism or taxon information when searching by gene symbol.
Use stable Gene IDs for repeatable lookups when they are already known.
Respect NCBI rate limits and use an API key only when higher request volume is needed.
anti_patterns: Do not treat gene symbols as globally unique across all organisms.
Do not use this skill for clinical decisions without qualified review.
Do not paste sensitive API keys into shared prompts, logs, or public notebooks.

faq.question: Which data source does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses official NCBI Gene access through E-utilities and NCBI Datasets API endpoints.

faq.question: Does it require an NCBI API key?
faq.answer: No. An API key is optional, but it increases allowed request rates for larger workflows.

faq.question: Can it process many genes at once?
faq.answer: Yes. The batch script accepts comma-separated Gene IDs or a file of gene symbols.

faq.question: Can it run without internet access?
faq.answer: No. It queries live NCBI services, so network access is required for retrieval.

faq.question: Does it analyze variants or expression data?
faq.answer: No. It retrieves gene records and metadata, but it does not perform variant or expression analysis.

faq.question: What should users verify in results?
faq.answer: Users should verify organism, Gene ID, symbol aliases, and record currency before using results in publications.
